---
title: "Step 16: Follow Up With Primary Intended Users to Facilitate and Enhance Use"
description: "Detailed study notes for Step 16: Follow Up With Primary Intended Users to Facilitate and Enhance Use in Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation."
book_id: "essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation"
collection_id: "evaluation-texts"
note_type: "Step"
step_number: 16
source_pages: "401-410"
citation_key: "patton_2012_essentials_utilization_focused_evaluation"
evidence_ids:
  - "chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:step16-step-16-follow-up-with-primary-intended-users-to-facilitate-and-enhance-use:c0004"
  - "chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:step16-step-16-follow-up-with-primary-intended-users-to-facilitate-and-enhance-use:c0011"
  - "chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:interlude-interconnections-among-steps-14-16-u-fe-as-a-complex-dynamic-system-fa:c0001"
source_status: "needs_review"
---

## Source

<div class="chapter-meta"><span>2012</span><span>MCP evaluation-texts</span><span>Status: needs_review</span><span>Step 16</span><span>Source pages 401-410</span></div>

This note summarises step 16, "Follow Up With Primary Intended Users to Facilitate and Enhance Use," from Michael Quinn Patton's *Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation* [@patton_2012_essentials_utilization_focused_evaluation]. It is a paraphrased study note based on source-linked MCP research-library retrieval records, not a substitute for the book section.

The retrieval trail is retained in the source records at the bottom of the page. The note paraphrases the section's argument and procedures, and it does not reproduce textbook tables, checklists, exhibits, graphics, or extended passages. When a checklist, exhibit, or graphic is central, the note describes its function and leaves the source artifact in the evidence record.

## Core Argument

Use often requires active follow-up after reporting, including planning, support, adaptation, and attention to emergent opportunities or resistance.

## Study Summary

Step 16 makes follow-up a core part of utilization-focused evaluation. The evaluator's responsibility does not necessarily end when the report is delivered, because intended use often requires continued facilitation.

Follow-up can include helping users interpret findings, adapt messages for new audiences, keep findings visible, respond to resistance, guard against misuse, and pursue emergent opportunities.

The chapter also makes follow-up a resource issue. If actual use matters, time and budget for post-report support should be negotiated rather than treated as an optional courtesy.

The interlude among Steps 14-16 shows feedback loops: follow-up may send users back to the report, additional analysis, revised displays, or new dissemination strategies.

For close reading, track how this section uses follow-up, intended use, reporting, and adaptive use while moving through follow-up plan, budget and time, and user support. The notes below identify what the section asks the evaluator to notice, what use problem it helps solve, and what should be checked against the saved evidence records before relying on the summary in applied work.

Within the wider book, this step belongs to the interpretation-and-use sequence: it moves from data sense-making to reporting, follow-up, and accountability for actual use.

## Key Concepts

- **follow-up**: Post-report support that keeps findings in front of users, adapts communication, and helps facilitate actual use.
- **intended use**: The specific way evaluation process or findings are expected to inform decisions, learning, action, or influence.
- **reporting**: Purposeful presentation of findings in formats, timing, and venues designed to facilitate use.
- **adaptive use**: Ongoing adjustment of evaluation questions, methods, communication, or follow-up as users, evidence, and context change.
- **evaluation facilitation**: The evaluator role of helping intended users clarify, negotiate, interpret, and act throughout the evaluation.

## Section Structure

- Follow-up plan.
- Budget and time.
- User support.
- Resistance and misuse.
- Feedback to reporting.

## Practical Implications

- Budget for follow-up before the evaluation closes.
- Keep findings in front of people positioned to use them.
- Treat follow-up as facilitation of use, not after-sales service.

## Concept Map

::: {.concept-map}
```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
  Problem["Problem: What use problem does Step 16 help solve?"]
  Central["Concept: Follow Up With Primary Intended Users to Facilitate and Enhance Use"]
  Concept1["Concept: follow-up"]
  Concept2["Concept: intended use"]
  Concept3["Concept: reporting"]
  Mechanism1["Mechanism: follow-up plan"]
  Mechanism2["Mechanism: budget and time"]
  Mechanism3["Mechanism: user support"]
  Implication1["Practice implication: Budget for follow-up before the evaluation closes."]
  Implication2["Practice implication: Keep findings in front of people positioned to use them."]
  Question["Open question: What would make this usable in a live evaluation?"]

  Problem --> Central
  Central --> Question
  Central --> Concept1
  Central --> Concept2
  Central --> Concept3
  Concept1 --> Mechanism1
  Concept2 --> Mechanism2
  Concept3 --> Mechanism3
  Mechanism1 --> Implication1
  Mechanism2 --> Implication2
  Implication1 --> Question

  classDef problem fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#b91c1c,color:#7f1d1d;
  classDef concept fill:#e6f3f1,stroke:#115e59,color:#134e4a;
  classDef mechanism fill:#e8f1ff,stroke:#1d4ed8,color:#1e3a8a;
  classDef implication fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#be185d,color:#831843;
  class Problem problem;
  class Central concept;
  class Concept1,Concept2,Concept3 concept;
  class Mechanism1,Mechanism2,Mechanism3 mechanism;
  class Implication1,Implication2,Question implication;
```
:::

## Connections

- Book overview: [Overview](index.qmd)
- Reading route: [Chapter Map](chapter-map.qmd)
- Concepts synthesis: [Core Concepts](concepts.qmd)
- Practice synthesis: [Practice Implications](practice-implications.qmd)
- Previous: [Step 15: Prepare an Evaluation Report to Facilitate Use and Disseminate Significant Findings to Expand Influence](step-15-prepare-report-to-facilitate-use-and-disseminate-findings.qmd)
- Next: [Step 17: Metaevaluation of Use: Be Accountable, Learn, and Improve](step-17-metaevaluation-of-use.qmd)

## Study Prompts

1. What use problem does step 16 help solve?
2. Which primary intended users would need to participate for this guidance to work?
3. How would this section change scoping, methods, interpretation, reporting, or follow-up?
4. Where could this guidance be misused if treated as a fixed template?

## Source Records

<details class="source-chunks">
<summary>Source records</summary>
<ul class="source-chunk-list">
<li><code>chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:step16-step-16-follow-up-with-primary-intended-users-to-facilitate-and-enhance-use:c0004</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:step16-step-16-follow-up-with-primary-intended-users-to-facilitate-and-enhance-use:c0011</code></li>
<li><code>chunk:essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation:interlude-interconnections-among-steps-14-16-u-fe-as-a-complex-dynamic-system-fa:c0001</code></li>
</ul>
</details>

## References

- Patton, M. Q. (2012). *Essentials of Utilization-Focused Evaluation*. SAGE Publications. Step 16: Follow Up With Primary Intended Users to Facilitate and Enhance Use, pages 401-410.
- Saved retrieval metadata: `data/research-library/evidence/essentials-utilization-focused-evaluation/step-evidence-summary.json`.
